title: Global number of oil spills from tankers
subtitle: Tanker accidents are broken down by magnitude based on the amount of oil spilled.
caption:
Global number of oil spills from tankers, 1970 to 2024
Tanker accidents are broken down by magnitude based on the amount of oil spilled.

This stacked bar chart tracks the annual count of tanker oil spills from 1970 through 2024, categorizing incidents into large spills exceeding 700 tonnes and medium spills between 7 and 700 tonnes. The vertical axis measures the number of spills, ranging from zero to 120, while the horizontal axis marks the timeline. The data shows a distinct downward trend over the fifty-four-year period, with the highest frequency of accidents occurring in the mid-1970s. In 1974, the total number of spills reached its maximum, with the combined height of the blue and maroon bars approaching 120 incidents; medium spills comprised the majority of this peak, while large spills accounted for roughly 25 of the total. The year 1975 followed closely with a similarly high volume of accidents. Through the late 1970s and 1980s, the annual totals fluctuated but generally remained between 40 and 90 incidents. A noticeable drop occurred around 1990, where the total count fell to approximately 65, followed by a period of further decline in the late 1990s and 2000s. By 2010, the annual total had decreased to fewer than 10 spills. The bars for the years 2014 through 2023 consistently show low values, often hovering between 5 and 10 total incidents per year. The final bar for 2024 shows a slight increase compared to the immediate preceding years, with the total count rising to approximately 10. The visual pattern displays a substantial reduction in the frequency of both medium and large tanker oil spills from the 1970s peak to the lower levels observed in the 2010s and 2020s.
